agent_raw: The six-week pilot cannot support the proposed network-wide expansion of the fraud model. Three confounds prevent a clean causal inference. First, merchant self-selection: pilot merchants volunteered and were 'mostly large retailers with dedicated risk teams.' This self-selected group is systematically different from the rest of the network — they have more sophisticated fraud management practices and likely different baseline fraud exposure profiles. Comparing their outcomes to the non-pilot remainder is comparing unlike groups. Second, concurrent authentication changes: some pilot merchants adopted stronger checkout authentication features during the same period. Better authentication directly reduces fraud independently of any scoring model. If the merchants with the most fraud improvement were also those who improved their authentication, the model's contribution cannot be isolated. Third, approved-only label observability: fraud labels exist only for approved transactions. If pilot merchants had different approval rates than non-pilot merchants (which is plausible since they could configure thresholds using model scores), the measured fraud rates reflect different slices of the transaction distribution. Comparing these rates without adjusting for approval-rate differences can be misleading. The same-calendar-week design controls for timing, not for these three structural confounds. A randomized or switchback test within comparable merchants with stable authentication settings and consistent approval tracking is required before network-wide expansion.
all_issues_raised: Merchant self-selection: pilot merchants are large sophisticated retailers unlike rest of network
Concurrent authentication changes confound fraud reduction attribution
Approved-only label observability differs by approval rate policy across merchant groups
